Next, the frenoesophageal ligament covering the right crust is divided so that we gain access to space between the, develop the plane between the oesophagus and the right crust of the diaphragm.
During this part of the dissection, it's very important to stay in the right plane because if we enter in between the right crust muscles, we will have an oozy field.
The retroesophageal space is entered and then further developed by anterior dissection of the oesophagus bluntly.
Once the retroesophageal plane is developed, we can see the posterior vagus.
It's easily identified as seen here.
It's a very thick structure, which is most of the time it's lying on the anterior surface of the aorta.
In some cases, it may sometimes be lying closer to the esophagus and may get lifted up with the esophagus during the retroesophageal dissection.
So we have to look for it in these two spaces.
Once the thick posterior vagus is identified, it is held with an instrument and
It is divided using either a bipolar or a harmonic.
After tackling the posterior vagus, the anterior vagus is more tricky.
The posterior vagus is usually a single thick nerve so it's easy to identify but the anterior vagus on the other hand, it could be a single nerve or two or more branches.

The best approach for the anterior vagotomy is once we divide the phrenoesophageal ligament like we've divided here, the best approach would be to completely bare the anterior and the left lateral surface of the esophagus till the esophageal muscle dividing all the nerve and the connective tissue.

Sometimes as in this patient which you see here, we may see a single thick anterior vagus nerve fiber which is usually anteriorly and seen to the left of midline because the left vagus actually essentially becomes the anterior vagus and the right nerve becomes the posterior vagus.
After completion of the vagotomy, we inspect all around the esophagus to look for any missed vagal trunks.

To prepare the stomach for the gastrogenostomy, one of the important steps which we do laparoscopically is to devascularize the part of the greater curve where we're going to site the gastrogenostomy.
So the area of the junction of the body and the antrum where we're going to actually site the GJ is being devascularized by staying close to the gastric wall.

this is because the stomach is a pretty thick wall structure with a very brisk vasculature and after stapling there's always from the staple dinastomosis days after stapling the stomach there's always a risk of bleeding from the staple line so for the staple gastrointestinal we would always do
devascularize the greater curve before doing the stapling.

This is not so important in the hands-free anastomosis because we'll be opening both the loops and then suturing them under vision, but still it's a fairly safe practice to follow while doing a laparoscopic gastrogynostomy, adequately devascularizing the greater curve at the site of the anastomosis.

Basically in this technique the Truncal Vagotomy is done essentially in the same fashion as in the first video because the main incision and the main difficulty in exposure we need is for the Truncal Vagotomy.

So the Truncal Vagotomy is done laparoscopically, the greater cover of the stomach is devascularized for the anastomosis, the mesocolon is lifted, the jejunal loop is identified and brought up to align with the stomach and the stomach and jejunum are being aligned here with a series of four seromuscular sutures.
So till this point both the surgeries are same but once the stomach and the jejunum is aligned and these four seromuscular stitches are taken we actually do the anastomosis extracorporeally.
So how we do it is basically at the completion of the fourth stitch we leave the thread very long the needle holder which is there in the right hand working instrument is going to help

hold this thread close to the anti-trap down wall then we make a small mini laparotomy in this case it's a very tiny 2 cm laparotomy through which we deliver the medial edge of the gastrointestinal anastomosis and make a small gastrotomy and enterotomy and put a stapler through that already the stomach is devascularized and they are aligned so we just fire the stapler and close the enterotomy and gastrotomy hand soon so this sort of the main incision which is required for the truncal vagotomy is avoided
and the loops are aligned and just the small incision is used for the laparotomy.

In case you don't want to use a stapler, rather than make a 2 cm incision, you can make about a 4 cm incision and deliver the aligned GJ loop through this incision and do the anastomosis extracorporeally.
Even this way, you'll be avoiding a much longer incision and the JJ can also be done in an exactly similar fashion.
